message:
fix(deps,bottlenecks): widen blocker-edge scope and fix bottleneck direction

Two bugs in the same dep-edge pipeline that bit the same downstream
consumers (stats summary, MCP bottleneck context/resource).

src/storage/sqlite.rs::get_blocks_dep_edges
  Previously the helper returned only edges of type `blocks`. Stats
  and the MCP bottleneck queries treat its output as "the set of
  edges that prevent forward progress", so blockers expressed via
  `conditional-blocks`, `waits-for`, or implied by a `parent-child`
  relationship all silently dropped out of the picture.
  Now the query unions four sources:
    - `blocks`              edges as-is
    - `conditional-blocks`  edges as-is
    - `waits-for`           edges as-is
    - `parent-child`        edges, with issue_id and depends_on_id
                            *swapped* (the parent depends on the
                            child finishing, not the other way
                            around)
  Same `(issue_id, depends_on_id)` shape as before, just with the
  full set of forward-blocking edges.

  Also drops `get_blocked_by_blocks_deps_only`, which is now
  redundant — the only callers were the stats tests, and the
  rewritten tests below don't need a separate "blocks-only" helper.

src/mcp/prompts.rs::bottleneck_context
src/mcp/resources.rs::compute_bottlenecks
  The for-loop destructured each edge as `(blocker, blocked)`, but
  `get_blocks_dep_edges` returns `(issue_id, depends_on_id)` — i.e.
  `(blocked, blocker)`. Result: the "blocks_count" map was keyed by
  the *blocked* issue, not the blocker, so the bottleneck list named
  the wrong issue as the bottleneck. Both call sites flip the
  destructure to `(blocked, blocker)` so the existing
  `blocks_count.entry(blocker.as_str())` increments the right key.

src/cli/commands/stats.rs (tests)
  Two stats tests previously called `get_blocked_by_blocks_deps_only`
  directly to assert "did t-2 end up blocked?". With that helper
  gone, they now build a row vector and run `compute_summary`, then
  assert on `summary.blocked_issues` (and `summary.ready_issues`
  in the active-blocker case). Same coverage, exercised through the
  same code path the CLI actually runs.
